text: Nature’s non-heme iron-containing enzymes catalyze important dioxygen-activating oxygenation reactions in biology through the formation of high-spin iron(IV)-oxo intermediates. Development of synthetic analogues of these iron enzymes has long been an active area of research, however activation of $\text{O}_2$ to form a high-spin iron species in the (S = 2) state has not been achievable in synthetic complexes and catalysis. We present here the design, synthesis and $\text{O}_2$ activation of an iron metal-organic framework material that enables low-temperature activation of $\text{O}_2$ with the iron centre to be reduced and then decarboxylate $\alpha$-keto acid co-ligands to form a high-spin iron(IV)=O species. These intermediates were characterized through in situ DRIFTS, in situ and variable-field room temperature (RT) Mössbauer, Fe K$\beta$ X-ray emission and nuclear resonance vibrational spectroscopies (NRVS). Catalyst activation and catalytic performance using the iron material is demonstrated through turnover of cyclohexane with $\text{O}_2$ as well as through stoichiometric oxidation of ethane into ethanol using $\text{O}_2$ as the oxidant. Here we therefore present a study that not only bridges enzymatic catalysis and heterogeneous materials, but also is able to produce the same robust high-spin reactivity seen in oxygenases in a stable porous solid.
